moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn, claiming central space and freeing both our bishop and queen for development. This classical move controls the d5 and f5 squares while preparing to build a strong pawn center. Our plan is to develop quickly and take advantage of the center, especially since many hypermodern defenses like what's coming will allow us to occupy it freely. The move also prepares us for various setups depending on how Black responds to our central challenge.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: 1.e4 claims the center, opens lines for the queen and bishop, and prepares to occupy d5 and f5.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black immediately signals their intention to play the Pterodactyl Defense, a hypermodern system that prioritizes piece development over immediate central occupation. This move prepares to fianchetto the dark-squared bishop on g7, where it will exert long-range pressure along the diagonal toward our kingside. Our opponent is essentially saying 'go ahead and take the center - I'll attack it from the flanks.' This defense can be tricky because it often leads to unbalanced positions where Black gets active piece play in exchange for giving us central space.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black fianchettoes the dark-squared bishop to g7, pressuring your center and kingside from the flank.

moveAnnotations.annotation: We gladly accept the invitation to establish a classical two-pawn center, now controlling key central squares like e5 and c5. This move completes our ideal pawn center and gives us excellent development prospects for our pieces. Against hypermodern defenses, occupying the center early is usually the right approach since our opponent has voluntarily allowed us this luxury. The d4 pawn also supports a future Nc3 and opens lines for our dark-squared bishop, giving us multiple development options.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: d4 completes the two-pawn center, controlling e5 and c5 while opening lines for the dark-squared bishop and Nc3.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto setup, placing their dark-squared bishop on its most active square where it eyes our central pawns and potentially our kingside. This bishop becomes the cornerstone of Black's position, often supporting counterplay with moves like ...c5 or ...d6 followed by ...f5. Our opponent is now ready to challenge our center from this strong diagonal, and we need to be aware that this bishop can become quite dangerous if we're not careful with our king safety. The Pterodactyl is starting to take shape as Black prepares to strike at our pawn center.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Black's fianchettoed dark-squared bishop eyes the center and kingside, supporting ...c5 or ...f5 to challenge White's pawns.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Nc3 supports the d4 pawn and prepares f4, a key plan to reinforce White's central space advantage before Black can consolidate with d6.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Nc3 is the main line against the Pterodactyl Defense Eastern Anhanguera.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at d4 immediately with c5, the typical counterattack in this structure. White must now decide whether to maintain the pawn or trade it away.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: c5 is the Pterodactyl Defense Eastern Anhanguera, a theory move challenging White's center.

moveAnnotations.annotation: Be3 keeps d4 defended and readies the kingside. This flexible setup preserves White's option to advance f4 or meet d6 depending on Black's next move.
moveAnnotations.shortNarration: Be3 is the theory move in the Pterodactyl Defense Eastern Anhanguera.
